chapter: Chapter 9: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

questions.question: Explain what a mixture is and distinguish between uniform and non-uniform mixtures.
questions.answer: A mixture is formed when two or more substances combine without undergoing a chemical change. In uniform mixtures, the components are evenly distributed and cannot be seen separately, while in non-uniform mixtures the components remain unevenly distributed and visible.

questions.question: Explain the terms solute, solvent, and solution with suitable examples.
questions.answer: A solution is a uniform mixture formed when a solute dissolves in a solvent. The solute is the substance that dissolves, while the solvent is the substance that dissolves the solute.

questions.question: Explain why air is considered a solution.
questions.answer: Air is considered a solution because it is a uniform mixture of gases. Nitrogen is present in the largest amount and acts as the solvent, while oxygen and other gases act as solutes.

questions.question: Explain the concept of saturated and unsaturated solutions.
questions.answer: An unsaturated solution is one in which more solute can dissolve at a given temperature. A saturated solution contains the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve, and any additional solute remains undissolved.

questions.question: Describe the experiment used to determine how much salt dissolves in water.
questions.answer: In this experiment, salt is gradually added to water and stirred until it dissolves. Eventually, a stage is reached where no more salt dissolves and the extra salt settles at the bottom, showing that the solution has become saturated.

questions.question: Explain the concept of solubility.
questions.answer: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a fixed quantity of solvent at a given temperature.

questions.question: Explain how temperature affects solubility of solids.
questions.answer: For most solid substances, solubility increases with temperature. Heating the solvent allows more particles of the solute to dissolve.

questions.question: Describe the experiment demonstrating the effect of temperature on solubility.
questions.answer: In the experiment, baking soda is dissolved in water at different temperatures. When the temperature increases, more baking soda dissolves, showing that solubility increases with temperature.

questions.question: Explain the solubility of gases in water.
questions.answer: Gases such as oxygen dissolve in water in small amounts. This dissolved oxygen supports aquatic organisms like fish and plants.

questions.question: Explain why aquatic organisms depend on dissolved oxygen.
questions.answer: Aquatic organisms such as fish use dissolved oxygen for respiration. Without sufficient oxygen in water, they cannot survive.

questions.question: Explain why some objects float while others sink in water.
questions.answer: Objects float or sink depending on their density compared to the density of water. Less dense objects float, while denser objects sink.

questions.question: Define density and explain its formula.
questions.answer: Density is the mass of a substance per unit volume. It is calculated using the formula Density = Mass ÷ Volume.

questions.question: Explain how the mass of an object can be measured.
questions.answer: Mass is measured using a balance, such as a digital weighing balance. The object is placed on the balance and the reading displayed gives its mass.

questions.question: Explain how the volume of a liquid can be measured.
questions.answer: The volume of a liquid can be measured using a measuring cylinder by reading the level of the liquid at the bottom of the meniscus.

questions.question: Explain how the volume of regular solids is calculated.
questions.answer: The volume of regular solids such as cuboids can be calculated using the formula length × width × height.

questions.question: Explain how the volume of irregular solids is determined.
questions.answer: The volume of irregular solids can be measured using the water displacement method. The increase in water level indicates the volume of the object.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between density and floating of objects.
questions.answer: Objects float if their density is lower than the density of the liquid and sink if their density is higher.

questions.question: Explain why hot air rises.
questions.answer: When air is heated, its particles move apart, increasing volume and decreasing density. Less dense hot air rises above cooler air.

questions.question: Explain how pressure affects the density of gases.
questions.answer: Increasing pressure compresses gas particles closer together, decreasing volume and increasing density.

questions.question: Explain why ice floats on water.
questions.answer: Ice floats on water because it is less dense than liquid water. When water freezes, its particles arrange themselves in a structure that occupies more space.

questions.question: Explain the concept of relative density.
questions.answer: Relative density compares the density of a substance with the density of water and has no unit.

questions.question: Explain the importance of measuring mass and volume in density calculations.
questions.answer: Density depends on mass and volume. Accurate measurements of these quantities are essential for calculating density correctly.

questions.question: Explain how density influences real-life applications such as boats and balloons.
questions.answer: Boats float because their overall density is less than water, while hot air balloons rise because heated air inside them is less dense than the surrounding air.

questions.question: Explain why oil floats on water.
questions.answer: Oil floats on water because its density is lower than the density of water.

questions.question: Explain the overall importance of understanding solutions and density in science.
questions.answer: Understanding solutions and density helps explain many natural and everyday phenomena such as dissolving substances, aquatic life survival, floating objects, and various industrial processes.
